MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DYLAN HACKETT, Esq. of The Hackett Law Firm (tel: 415-410-9931) for Plaintiff.
Atty. GABRIELA GONZALEZ, Esq. of Narayan Travelstead Ku, P.C. (tel: 650-403-0150) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Monetary Sanctions.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

On December 13, 2024, Defendant the Regents of the University of California's unopposed motion to strike punitive damages from the second amended complaint, was granted without leave to amend. Defendant now seeks $3,530 in monetary sanctions from Plaintiff Elizabeth Hsu and her counsel for improperly seeking punitive damages in her Third Amended Complaint ("TAC"); that punitive damages claim has now also been stricken.

Defendant sent a meet and confer letter on January 14, 2025, informing Plaintiff of its intent to bring the instant motion. Defendant then filed the motion with the court on January 27, 2025. On February 12, 2025, the parties agreed to have the hearing continued to April 9, 2025.

CCP 128.7(b)(1) provides that notice of motion shall be served as provided in Section 1010, but shall not be filed with or presented to the court unless, within 21 days after service of the motion, or any other period as the court may prescribe, the challenged paper, claim, defense, contention, allegation, or denial is not withdrawn or appropriately corrected.

Defendant failed to provide Plaintiff with a notice of motion which complies with Section 1010 (See Defendant's Ex. 7), 21 days before filing the motion for sanctions with the court. Meet and confer efforts are not the same as the formal presentment of the motion itself, as required by the statute. (Cf. Broadcast Music, Inc. v. Structured Asset Sales, LLC (2022) 75 Cal.App.5th 596, 605 ["We conclude that the statute is unambiguous. A sanctions motion cannot be filed until the 22nd day after service of the motion, i.e., after the 21-day safe harbor period expires."].)

The sanctions motion is denied. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied.

Plaintiff's counsel is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

10:08am The Court passes the matter at the first calling of the case since Defendant's counsel is not present.

10:34 The matter is recalled. Plaintiff's counsel is not contesting the Demurrer. Mr. Quattrociocche contest the motion to strike only. Plaintiff's counsel clarifies the court's ruling as to the punitive damges. The Court shares its views. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

The demurrer to the second amended complaint is SUSTAINED WITH 15 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND.

Exhaustion of administrative remedies is a "jurisdictional prerequisite to resort to the courts." (Ortega v. Contra Costa Community College Dist. (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 1073, 1081.) Under FEHA, a party must file a timely administrative complaint with the CRD and obtain a "right-to-sue" notice. (Government Code sections 12960, 12965(c).) The failure to file an administrative charge before commencing suit is ground for dismissal of the FEHA claim. (Martin v. Lockheed Missiles & Space Co., Inc. (1994) 29 Cal.App.4th 1718, 1724.) Plaintiff must plead compliance with the administrative requirements. (Hood v. Hacienda La Puente Unified Sch. Dist. (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 435, 439 ["Plaintiff had a duty to plead exhaustion of administrative remedies or facts which indicate the duty to do so has been excused."]; Williams v. Housing Authority of Los Angeles (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 708, 736 [demurrer sustained where Plaintiff's complaint failed to allege satisfaction of administrative exhaustion requirement].) Here, plaintiff fails to plead facts that could support a finding she exhausted her administrative remedies.

Additionally, as to the First, Second and Third causes of action, the second amended complaint fails to plead facts sufficient to state FEHA claims. Plaintiff must, for example, identify her disability, describe substantially limited life activities, identify accommodation requests and related activities and identify facts sufficient to support inferences of age and race discrimination.

Plaintiff is granted 15 days leave to amend.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court reporter: Brianna Rudd, CSR #13668, 209-747-1306, briannarudd@gmail.com, Reported. (301/JMQ)

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrer to Elizabeth Hsu's four-page first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained with leave to amend. The FAC is too skimpy even by California's lenient standards. It first seeks to plead "failure to accommodate disability" based on hand and hip surgeries in 2019 and 2022. (FAC 2:11-13.) But a physical condition must "limit[] a major life activity" to be a disability, and the FAC does not suggest the surgeries were other than transitory. (Govt. Code 12926(m)(B); CACI 2541(3).) The FAC next seeks to plead age and "racial or national origin" discrimination on little more than Hsu's 40+ status and "Asian ancestry." (Id. at 3:7, 24.) Finally, the FAC's refusal-to-rehire "retaliation" theory merits more than the one sentence accorded it. (Id. at 4:15-17.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
